bibliographic_data.title: A Tool for Rejuvenating Feature Logging Levels via Git Histories and Degree of Interest
bibliographic_data.abstract: Logging is a significant programming practice. Due to the highly transactional nature of modern software applications, massive amounts of logs are generated every day, which may overwhelm developers. Logging information overload can be dangerous to software applications. Using log levels, developers can print the useful information while hiding the verbose logs during software runtime. As software evolves, the log levels of logging statements associated with the surrounding software feature implementation may also need to be altered. Maintaining log levels necessitates a significant amount of manual effort. In this paper, we demonstrate an automated approach that can rejuvenate feature log levels by matching the interest level of developers in the surrounding features. The approach is implemented as an open-source Eclipse plugin, using two external plug-ins (JGit and Mylyn). It was tested on 18 open-source Java projects consisting of ∼3 million lines of code and ∼4K log statements. Our tool successfully analyzes 99.22% of logging statements, increases log level distributions by ∼20%, and increases the focus of logs in bug fix contexts ∼83% of the time.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes an automated approach, REFELL, that rejuvenates feature log levels by matching the interest level of developers in surrounding features.
core_content.key_methodology: The approach uses Git histories for code changes and adapts Mylyn’s Degree of Interest (DOI) model to gauge developers' interest in the software source code surrounding logging statements. It is implemented as an open-source Eclipse plugin using JGit and Mylyn.
outcomes: Increases the focus of logs in bug fix contexts ∼83% of the time
core_content.full_text_markdown: Logging is a widely used programming practice for recording software system information during runtime. The evolution of modern software development has led to the generation of massive amounts of logs, which can overwhelm developers. This paper proposes an automated approach called REFELL that rejuvenates feature log levels by matching the interest level of developers in surrounding features. The approach uses Git histories for code changes and adapts Mylyn’s Degree of Interest (DOI) model to gauge developers' interest in the software source code surrounding logging statements. It is implemented as an open-source Eclipse plugin using JGit and Mylyn, tested on 18 open-source Java projects consisting of ∼3 million lines of code and ∼4K log statements. The tool successfully analyzes 99.22% of logging statements, increases log level distributions by ∼20%, and increases the focus of logs in bug fix contexts ∼83% of the time.
